key_insight: A new damage-and-fracture constitutive model enables finite-element prediction of failure in additively manufactured polymeric TPMS lattices under complex loading.
engineering_relevance: The framework improves the ability to design architected polymer lattices with better reliability, energy absorption, and failure resistance before fabrication.
method: Implemented a high-fidelity viscoplastic constitutive model in Abaqus/Explicit via user material subroutine.
Introduced a damage initiation criterion for amorphous polymers based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain.
Used a gradient-damage formulation to simulate fracture and validate against experiments.
evidence_signal: The model was validated against systematic experiments on TPMS unit cells and lattices under tension, compression, and non-monotonic loading, including quantitative and qualitative agreement for a unit cell and tensile lattice tests.
watch_item: The approach depends on calibrated material parameters and user-subroutine implementation in Abaqus, so transferability to other polymers, print processes, or loading rates may require revalidation.
paper_title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices
